items.myth: Tree models never need one-hot encoding because they handle categories natively.
items.reality: Classic CART implementations require numeric inputs—strings must be encoded. Some modern libraries accept categorical dtypes, but arbitrary label integers on nominal data still create misleading split order unless one-hot or native category support is used.
items.example: sklearn DecisionTreeClassifier on `color` encoded 0/1/2 may split `color <= 1`, grouping red and blue together with no semantic justification.

items.myth: Target encoding is always better than one-hot for high cardinality.
items.reality: Target encoding trades dimensionality for leakage risk and variance on rare levels; without cross-fitted statistics and smoothing it overfits and fails in production.
items.example: A zip code with one training sale gets encoded as that single house's exact price—memorization, not a neighborhood signal.

items.myth: One-hot columns can be dropped at random without affecting linear models.
items.reality: Dropping one dummy column (reference category) avoids the dummy-variable trap in OLS; dropping an arbitrary column shifts the intercept interpretation. All-zero rows for unseen categories are not the same as the reference level unless you define them that way.
items.example: With columns for red and blue only (green reference), an unseen yellow maps to (0,0)—not equivalent to green unless you route yellow to a known bucket.
